messages.content: I have 2 .csv files, one having data within "" and another having data without "". When I open these in Excel, it tampers the data within the "" csv file while the other file data is read as is without any tampering. How to ensure my csv with data in inverted quotes "" is read properly in Excel without any formatting

issues?

One way to ensure that the data within "" quotes is read properly in Excel without any formatting issues is to open Excel, select the Data tab, and then click on the From Text/CSV option. Choose the csv file with data within "", and then select the option to import the data as text. This will ensure that Excel does not interpret any of the data within "" as a formula or value and instead, keeps it as a text value. By importing it as text, the inverted quotes will be preserved, and the data will not be tampered with.
How to open a .csv file in excel, making sure that data in csv present within "" will not be formatted and read as it is ?

Here are the steps to open a .csv file in Excel, making sure that data in CSV present within "" will not be formatted and read as it is:

1. Open Microsoft Excel on your computer.
2. From the Excel ribbon, select the Data tab.
3. Click on the From Text/CSV button in the Get & Transform Data section.
4. Navigate to the folder where your .csv file is saved and select it.
5. Choose the required file type in the Import Text File dialog box and click on Import.
6. In the Text Import Wizard dialog box, select the Delimited option and click on Next.
7. Ensure that the Comma delimiter is selected and the Text qualifier is set to ". Then click on Next.
8. Select the data column in the Data preview section and choose Text in the Column data format options (if you want to preserve the information as text format in Excel).
9. Click on Finish to complete the import process. 

These steps will allow you to open a .csv file in Excel while ensuring that data in CSV present within "" will not be formatted and read as is. The inverted commas would be preserved, and the data within them will be read as text and not formulated by Excel into a different format.
Upon double clicking a csv file having data within "", it opens in Excel but alters the date fields. How to ensure the values are not formatted and read as it is ?

When you double-click a csv file having data within "", Excel tries to open and interpret the data as it would with any other spreadsheet. As a result, it may cause it to alter the date fields and convert them to a different format. To ensure that the values are not formatted and read as is, follow the steps below.

1. Open Microsoft Excel on your computer.
2. From the Excel ribbon, click on the File tab, and then click on Options.
3. In the Excel Options dialog box, click on the Advanced tab.
4. Scroll down to the General section and uncheck the option "Automatically format text links."
5. Click on OK to save changes and close the dialog box.
6. Exit Excel.

Now try to open the CSV file by double-clicking it. This time, the data in inverted quotes should be preserved and not formatted by Excel. If you still want to open the file via Excel avoiding the double-clicking way, you can follow the steps mentioned in my previous answers.

Cannot find the above, 
Scroll down to the General section and uncheck the option “Automatically format text links.”

I apologize for the confusion in my previous answer. The option "Automatically format text links" may not be available in all versions of Excel. Here's an updated method that should work for all versions.

1. Open Microsoft Excel on your computer.
2. From the Excel ribbon, click on the Data tab.
3. Click on "From Text/CSV" in the Get & Transform Data section.
4. Navigate to the folder where your .csv file is saved and select it.
5. Choose "Delimited" file type in the Import Text File dialog box, then click on "Next".
6. Select the delimiter type (e.g., comma) in the Delimiters section, then click "Next".
7. Select the column that contains data within "" and change the column data format to "Text" format (if you want to preserve the information as text format in Excel).
8. Click on "Finish" to complete the import process.

With these steps, you can import your CSV file and make sure that data in CSV present within ""will not be formatted and read as is without any formatting issues.
how to make sure Excel opens csv files without formatting the values in the file upon double clicking the csv ?

When you double-click a CSV file in Windows, it automatically opens in Excel, which tries to interpret the data and apply formatting based on its own rules. To make sure Excel opens CSV files without formatting the values in the file, you need to change the default program used to open CSV files.

Here are the steps to change the default program for opening CSV files on Windows:

1. Right-click on a CSV file and select "Open with" from the context menu.
2. If Excel is listed, select it and make sure the "Always use this app to open .csv files" box is checked. If Excel is not listed, click on "Choose another app" to find it.
3. Click "More apps" to see a list of all available programs.
4. Select Excel and make sure the "Always use this app to open .csv files" box is checked.
5. Click "OK" to set Excel as the default program for opening CSV files.

Now, when you double-click on a CSV file, Excel will open the file without applying any formatting to the values in the file. You can then format the data in Excel as needed.
Upon double clicking a csv file and opening in Excel, how to ensure that the values within "" in the csv are read as it is and not tampered or formatted by Excel ?

When you double-click a CSV file to open it in Excel, the data within quotes may be read and formatted by Excel based on its own rules. To ensure that values in quotes are read as is, you can follow the steps below.

1. Open a blank Excel workbook.
2. Click on the "Data" tab in the Excel ribbon and then click on "From Text/CSV" in the "Get & Transform Data" section.
3. In the Import Text File dialog box, navigate to the directory where the CSV file is saved, select the file, and click "Import".
4. In the Text Import Wizard dialog box, make sure that "Delimited" is selected and click "Next".
5. In the Delimiters section, select the delimiter used in the CSV file (e.g., comma) and click "Next".
6. In the Data preview section, select the column(s) containing values within quotes, click on "Text" in the "Column data format" field, and then click "Finish".
7. In the Import Data dialog box, select the desired location for the imported data (e.g., a new worksheet), and then click "OK".
8. Excel will now import and display the data from the CSV file without altering or formatting values within quotes.

By importing the CSV file using the "From Text/CSV" option in Excel, you can ensure that the values within quotes are read and displayed as is without any formatting or changes made by Excel.
Upon double clicking a csv file having data within “”, it opens in Excel but alters the date fields. How to ensure the values are not formatted and read as they are present in csv ?

When you double-click a CSV file in Windows, Excel automatically opens the file and tries to interpret the data based on its own rules, including formatting date fields. To ensure that the values in the CSV file are not formatted and read as they are, you can follow the steps below:

1. Open a blank Excel workbook.
2. Select the "Data" tab in the Excel ribbon and then click on "From Text/CSV" in the "Get & Transform Data" section.
3. Navigate to the folder where your CSV file is saved and select the file.
4. Choose the "Delimited" file type in the "Import Text File" dialog box and click "Next."
5. Select the delimiter used in the CSV file (e.g. comma) and click "Next."
6. Select the column(s) containing the date fields and change the data format of those columns to "Text" instead of "Date."
7. Click "Finish" to complete the import process.
8. In the import data dialog box, select the desired location for the imported data (e.g. a new worksheet).

By importing the CSV file using the "From Text/CSV" option in Excel and setting the format of the date columns to "Text," you can ensure that Excel does not automatically format or alter the date fields in the CSV file. Excel will display the date fields exactly as they are present in the CSV file.

Upon double clicking a csv file having data within “”, it opens in Excel but alters the date fields. How to ensure the values are not formatted and read as it is ?
I do not want to import from within Excel, I'll double click on csv and it opens in Excel.

When you double-click a CSV file with data within “”, Excel automatically opens and displays the file contents. By default, Excel will try to infer the format of the data in the CSV file and may alter the date fields. However, you can take the following steps to prevent Excel from altering the data:

1. Open Microsoft Excel on your computer.
2. Click on the File tab in the Excel ribbon, and then select Options.
3. Click on the Advanced tab in the Excel Options dialog box.
4. Scroll down to the "When calculating this workbook" section and select the "Use system separators" option.
5. Click on the OK button to confirm and save the changes.

By selecting the "Use system separators" option, Excel will use the default settings on your system for displaying and interpreting data in the CSV file, including the date fields. In most cases, this will prevent Excel from altering the data when you double-click on the CSV file to open it.

If you want to format the data specifically as text, you can also follow these additional steps:

6. Select the column(s) that contain the data within inverted commas ("").
7. Right-click on the selection and choose "Format cells" from the context menu.
8. In the Format Cells dialog box, select the "Text" format and click on OK.

By formatting the data as text, you can further ensure that Excel does not apply any formatting or alter the data when you open the CSV file.

Upon double clicking a csv file with data within "" and opening in Excel,  Excel is autoformatting the fields and changing the values. How to ensure Excel does no formatting and reads it as it is ? Preserves all the data intact and all cell should be in General format

When you double-click a CSV file with data within "", Excel automatically opens and tries to format and infer the data types based on its own rules. However, you can take the following steps to ensure that Excel does not format the data and preserves it in the General format:

1. Open Microsoft Excel on your computer.
2. Click on the File tab in the Excel ribbon, and then select Options.
3. Click on the Advanced tab in the Excel Options dialog box.
4. Scroll down to the "Editing options" section and remove the checkmark from the "Automatically insert a decimal point" option.
5. Scroll down to the "When calculating this workbook" section and select the "Set precision as displayed" option.
6. Scroll down to the "Lotus compatibility" section and select the "Transition formula entry" option.
7. Click on the OK button to confirm and save the changes.

By doing these settings, you can ensure that all cells in the CSV file do not contain any specific formatting and are displayed in general format. All values will be displayed in their original form without any alteration. You may need to reopen the CSV file for the changes to take effect.

If you still find that Excel is formatting the data, you can try importing the CSV file using the "From Text/CSV" option in Excel as described in the previous solution. Alternatively, you can also open the CSV file within Excel, highlight the column(s) containing data within "", right-click, and select "Format Cells." Then under the "Number" tab, select "General" and click "OK." This will convert any incorrectly formatted cells back to general format.

Double clicking a .csv file with data within "" and opening in Excel, Excel is formatting the cell values and changing automatically. I want all Cell format to be General no matter what and all the field values should remain as they are. 
Date separated by - should not be separated by /

To ensure that the cell format of all cells remains as General while opening a .csv file in Excel, and that the field values are not altered, including the dates separated by -, you can follow the steps below:

1. Right-click on the .csv file you want to open and select "Open with" from the context menu.
2. Select "Excel" from the list of applications, and make sure the "se Do this for all .csv files" box is checked.
3. Click "OK" to open the file in Excel.
4. Excel may display a 'Text Import Wizard' dialog box, select "Delimited" and select the appropriate delimiter and check "Treat consecutive delimiters as one."
5. In the 'Text Import Wizard' dialog box, select all columns and choose "Text" under "Column data format."
6. Make sure no columns are highlighted and click on "Finish."
7. Now selecting the respective date columns, you need to perform a search and replace to change - to /.
8. Press "Ctrl + H" or select "Replace" under the "Find & Select" section of the "Home" tab.
9. In the "Replace" dialog box, enter "-" in the "Find what" field, enter "/" in the "Replace with" field, and click on "Replace All."
10. Excel will replace the hyphens with forward slashes, and the date fields will no longer be separated incorrectly.

By following these steps, Excel will not make any formatting changes to the data, and all cells will be in the General format. Additionally, the dates separated by - will be corrected to / and displayed correctly.
